findings.recommendation: Add a feature for Human-on-the-Loop Escalation. Define decision thresholds (e.g., confidence < 0.7 → escalate), UI/UX for handover, timeout policies, and integration with the evidence store. Test with actual operators in Phase 4.

findings.finding_title: ODD coverage shortfall (<95%) requires synthetic data gap filling
findings.outcome_description: When coverage of declared ODD states is <95%, the test architecture identifies coverage gaps and routes a regeneration request to the synthetic data pipeline (NVIDIA TAO / StyleGAN / diffusion), with gap tags appended to the traceability matrix.
findings.triggering_scenario: After Phase 3 evaluation, coverage gap analysis shows <95% coverage in identified ODD cells (e.g., fog at night + high sea state + small target).
findings.reasoning: Acceptance criterion defines 95% as the must-achieve target. Gap closing is essential for AMLAS evidence completeness and traceability chain.
findings.recommendation: Implement automated coverage gap detector. Classify gaps by: criticality (safety-relevant vs. nice-to-have), effort (sim-time to generate), and ODD cell signature.

findings.finding_title: Calibration check fails; model confidence threshold adjusted or retrain triggered
findings.outcome_description: Phase 3 evaluation detects poor calibration (e.g., model assigns 90% confidence to predictions with only 75% empirical accuracy). Calibration failure triggers either: (a) confidence threshold post-hoc adjustment (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) with evidence of impact, or (b) model retrain with focal loss or temperature scaling.
findings.triggering_scenario: Held-out test set evaluation shows calibration error (ECE / MCE) > acceptable threshold (e.g., 5%).
findings.reasoning: Calibration is critical for closed-loop collision-avoidance: miscalibrated confidence can lead to unsafe trust-of-AI decisions. Requirements specify 'calibration' as a Phase 3 metric but do not define failure path.
findings.recommendation: Define calibration acceptance threshold (e.g., ECE < 3%). On failure: try post-hoc fix first (faster), measure impact on robustness, document in evidence. If post-hoc insufficient, trigger retrain with calibration loss.

findings.finding_title: Red-team adversarial scenario uncovers edge case; test case added to coverage
findings.outcome_description: Garak / PyRIT red-teaming identifies an adversarial scenario (e.g., lens flare + fast-moving small target + high sea state) where model confidence is high but accuracy is low. The scenario is logged, a synthetic data generation request is issued to StyleGAN/diffusion, new test cases are added to Phase 3, and the traceability matrix is updated with a new requirement node (e.g., 'REQ-EDGE-CASE-0047').
findings.triggering_scenario: Phase 3 red-teaming suite generates ~100 adversarial scenario mutations. One causes model failure. Threat modeller flags it.
findings.reasoning: Red-teaming is explicitly required but no feedback loop is defined. Edge case discovery should drive coverage closure and improve evidence robustness.
findings.recommendation: Implement red-teaming → evidence loop: tag discovered edge cases with 'root cause' (e.g., training data gap, model architecture limitation), generate synthetic samples, retrain, re-evaluate. Measure loop closure time.

findings.finding_title: Physical sea trial data analysis reveals sim-to-real gap; synthetic data regeneration prioritized
findings.outcome_description: Phase 5 targeted physical trials anchor synthetic evidence to real weather, comms, and UK littoral traffic. Real-world data analysis identifies a sim-to-real mismatch (e.g., model overconfident on real fishing nets vs. synthetic), triggering a gap analysis. Gap is tagged in the evidence store, StyleGAN/diffusion rendering is tuned to better match real sensor characteristics, and Phase 3 evaluation is re-run on augmented synthetic data.
findings.triggering_scenario: Phase 5 physical trials in BUTEC or Portland Harbour. Model detects fishing nets with 40% false negative rate on real data vs. 5% on synthetic data. Analysis links gap to sensor noise + rendering fidelity.
findings.reasoning: Phase 5 is explicitly about closing sim-to-real gap, but no automated feedback loop is defined. Gap closure is essential for evidence defensibility.
findings.recommendation: After sea trials: (1) run model on real data, (2) compare metrics to synthetic baseline, (3) identify classes/conditions with >5% gap, (4) conduct root-cause analysis (rendering, sensor simulation, lighting), (5) regenerate synthetic data, (6) re-train model, (7) document loop in evidence store.

findings.finding_title: Drift monitor SLO boundary at ±10% creates ambiguity in escalation timing
findings.feature: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
findings.scenario_text: Given deployment monitoring 3 ODD variables (visibility, sea state, illumination) with SLO ±10% from baseline, when visibility mean drifts from 300 m to 310 m (at 95% CI confidence interval boundary), then the system issues an SLO-yellow alert indicating marginal breach, and if the drift persists for >2 hours, escalates to SLO-red and triggers model snapshot checkpoint.
findings.scenario_data:
| Variable | Baseline | Observed | Delta | CI (95%) | Alert Level |
|----------|----------|----------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Visibility | 300 m | 310 m | +3.3% | Within ±10% | Yellow |
| Visibility (Persistent) | 300 m | 315 m (2+ hrs) | +5% | Crossing boundary | Red → Checkpoint |
findings.reasoning: SLO thresholds at hard boundaries (±10%) create decision ambiguity near the threshold. Lack of hysteresis or persistence logic can cause alert flapping or delayed escalation during marginal conditions.
findings.recommendation: Implement hysteresis and persistence logic in drift detection: (1) SLO-yellow after >1 violation, (2) SLO-red only after >N consecutive 15-min windows above threshold, (3) auto-escalate to checkpoint after >2 hours at SLO-red. Document escalation decision tree in monitoring policy.

findings.finding_title: Evidence traceability broken after tool migration — rebuild RTM or accept gap?
findings.scenario: Traceability matrix is maintained in Tool A (e.g., Doors). After Phase 3 evaluation, team migrates to Tool B (e.g., Jira). Test cases generated in Phase 3 were linked to requirement IDs in Tool A. After migration, 20% of links cannot be automatically mapped to Tool B. What is the action?
findings.realistic_alternative: Migration triggers a full traceability rebuild: all test cases are manually re-linked to requirements in new tool; missing links are escalated as coverage gaps; no evidence can be frozen until all links are restored
findings.absurd_alternative: System auto-maps as many links as possible (80%); marks 20% as 'legacy links pending resolution'; evidence is frozen and sent to Article 36 review anyway, with note 'partial traceability maintained'
findings.reasoning: Requirements mandate 'All AI-generated tests traceable to a named requirement' but do not address: (1) what happens when traceability is broken (tool failure, migration, data loss), (2) acceptable percentage of broken links before evidence is rejected, (3) remediation path (rebuild, accept, escalate). A developer must guess whether to block certification or accept partial traceability.
findings.recommendation: Implement traceability assurance: (1) before evidence freeze, validate 100% of test cases are linked to exactly 1 requirement, (2) during tool migrations, implement validation script that checks link integrity before and after migration, (3) on migration, any broken links must be manually re-verified or tests must be re-generated, (4) no evidence can be frozen if <100% traceability is confirmed, (5) any gap is escalated to Safety Review Board with impact assessment

findings.finding_title: Transition and decommissioning: no plan for migrating to future assurance frameworks or sunsetting the pipeline when AMLAS is superseded
findings.what_is_missing: Requirements assume AMLAS compliance as the target regulatory framework (Section 5, 6) but do not address: (1) what happens when UK Defence adopts a new autonomy assurance standard (post-AMLAS); (2) migration path for evidence (can AMLAS evidence be translated to future framework?); (3) decommissioning USV and retiring evidence (how long is evidence retained after USV retires?); (4) sunsetting the pipeline itself (if PSRQ becomes obsolete, how are teams transitioned?); (5) knowledge retention (is AMLAS expertise documented, or does institutional knowledge walk out the door?).
findings.where_expected: Section 7 PSRQ phases (missing Phase 8 'Transition & Decommissioning'). Section 10 non-AI enablers (missing 'Exit Strategy' subsection).
findings.impact: If no migration plan exists, a regulatory framework change could invalidate the entire evidence bundle and require rework. Evidence retention uncertainty creates legal risk (unknown whether evidence must be kept indefinitely or can be destroyed). Without decommissioning procedures, teams are left maintaining zombie pipelines or abandoning them abruptly. Institutional knowledge loss slows future autonomy programmes.
findings.reasoning: No analyst identified this forward-looking gap. Current requirements are silent on lifecycle end-states and framework evolution. This is a strategic/governance gap rather than functional, but critical for long-term programme sustainability.
findings.recommendation: Add Section 7.8 Transition & Decommissioning and Section 10.4 Exit Strategy: (a) Framework evolution plan: if UK Defence issues new autonomy assurance standard (post-AMLAS), PSRQ evidence must be re-mapped within 6 months. Dstl Autonomy Lab maintains a 'standards roadmap' document (updated annually) tracking emerging frameworks (e.g., ISO/SAE 5469 for AV, NIST AI RMF for AI systems). At 18-month warning before current AMLAS EOL, PSRQ governance creates rework task (estimate effort, schedule); (b) Evidence migration: AMLAS-structured GSN and evidence bundle are designed to be 'translatable' to future frameworks. Use of semantic standards (SPDX for provenance, SBOM for tool inventory, RDF for evidence links) reduces migration friction. At framework change, Dstl funds mapping study (≤4 weeks) to assess what evidence is still valid, what must be re-generated; (c) Evidence retention & archival: classified synthetic data (MOD DAIS) retained ≤5 years after model retirement (regulatory compliance + historical audit). Evidence bundle (traceability matrix, GSN, robustness bounds) retained indefinitely in a read-only archive (important for future appeal/audit of decisions made). Destroy/retention decisions are made by RAISO + Dstl Chief of Staff with 1-year notice (document justification). (d) Equipment decommissioning: when a USV platform retires, in-service monitoring ceases (Phase 7 ends). Drift monitors turned off. Final evidence bundle archived with timestamp and 'end-of-life' note. Incident history (e.g., 'drift detected, model updated') preserved for future reference. (e) Team transition: upon PSRQ completion or framework change, document 'lessons learned' (what worked, what was painful, what would we do differently?). Host 2-day knowledge transfer workshop with next-generation autonomy programme team. Create PSRQ runbook (reusable playbook for running PSRQ on future USV variants). (f) Sunsetting the pipeline: if PSRQ tools become obsolete (e.g., Evidently AI discontinued, replaced by new vendor), no requirement to migrate in-service monitoring immediately. However, if critical bug or security vulnerability is discovered in obsolete tool, Dstl funds 'tool swap' (point estimate ≤4 weeks) to migrate to alternative. For next USV programme, use updated tool set (current versions, actively maintained).

findings.finding_title: Dependency on external programmes and regulatory milestones: no explicit linkage to Project Cabot, UK Defence AI Playbook, or SACE adoption timelines
findings.what_is_missing: Requirements reference 'Alignment with JSP 520, JSP 936, DEFSTAN 00-056, RA 1600 and the UK Defence AI Playbook' (Section 10) and 'NATO STANAG / AUKUS Pillar II autonomy workstrand' but do not specify: (1) dependencies on Project Cabot (UK MOD strategic autonomy programme); (2) UK Defence AI Playbook milestone dates (when does AMLAS adoption deadline occur?); (3) SACE adoption timeline (is SACE now mandatory? When does it replace legacy AMLAS?); (4) critical path (does USV perception testing feed into Cabot decision gates?); (5) integration points (does PSRQ output evidence to Cabot steering group?); (6) schedule risk if external programme slips.
findings.where_expected: Section 10 non-AI enablers (should include 'Dependencies & Programme Alignment' subsection with explicit linkages to Cabot milestones and Playbook adoption dates).
findings.impact: If Project Cabot schedule slips, PSRQ may have completed evidence with nowhere to go (Cabot not ready to ingest). If UK Defence AI Playbook mandates SACE adoption in 2027 but PSRQ is still AMLAS-only, evidence must be re-structured (rework). External programme dependencies create schedule risk and coordination cost. Without explicit linkage, programme managers are unaware of upstream dependencies.
findings.reasoning: No analyst identified external-programme dependencies. Requirements are self-contained in an apparent vacuum, with only policy references (JSP, DEFSTAN) but no strategic programme links. This is a governance/planning gap for a programme that is embedded in a larger defence-capability ecosystem.
findings.recommendation: Add Section 10.5 Dependencies & External Programme Alignment: (a) Project Cabot alignment: USV perception testing is a workstream within Project Cabot (UK MOD's strategic autonomy research programme). PSRQ produces evidence bundles that feed into Cabot capability review gates (approx. Cabot decision gates: FY2026 Q3, FY2027 Q1, FY2027 Q4). Critical path: PSRQ must produce final GSN + Article 36 approval by Cabot gate minus 6 weeks (for Cabot steering group review). Schedule risk: if Cabot gates slip, PSRQ deadlines may slip (dependency documented in JIRA board). (b) UK Defence AI Playbook alignment: Playbook mandates AMLAS compliance by EOY2026 for all defence AI acquisition. This programme targets EOY2026 completion. If Playbook mandates SACE adoption in 2027 (currently under review), PSRQ artefacts must be re-mapped to SACE by end-2027 (rework budget: est. 4–6 engineer-weeks). Contingency: allocate 10% schedule buffer in Phase 6 for potential re-baselining. (c) SACE adoption roadmap: monitor UK Defence announcements for mandatory SACE adoption date. If adopted while PSRQ is in Phase 3–4, evaluate impact: (i) if minor (SACE is GSN + additional metadata), integrate incrementally; (ii) if major (SACE requires rework of evidence structure), request timeline extension from stakeholders. (d) NATO STANAG compliance: alignment is a must-have (not dependency), but if NATO changes STANAG definitions (e.g., redefines 'robustness'), evidence may need re-interpretation. Annual STANAG review (Q1) flags changes; if substantive, trigger evidence-rebaselining task. (e) Milestone synchronisation: create integrated schedule chart linking PSRQ phases to Cabot gates, Playbook deadlines, and STANAG review cycles. Shared with programme steering group (quarterly). Flag any schedule compression or overlap risks. (f) Handoff to operations: PSRQ Phase 7 (in-service monitoring) assumes USV has been operationally deployed by Cabot. If Cabot deployment slips, Phase 7 start date slips (no live platform to monitor). Contingency: Phase 7 can begin in simulation (drift detection on synthetic data) before real deployment, but real deployment is the true start.

findings.finding_title: Pairwise scenario coverage achieves 10× productivity gain but misses 3-way interactions
findings.feature: OpenSCENARIO 2.0 and Scenic scenario definition and automation
findings.scenario_text: Given Scenic defines encounters across sea-state ∈ {Beaufort 2,3,4}, visibility ∈ {50 m,200 m,500 m}, sensor ∈ {radar, lidar, visual}, when the scenario generator applies pairwise (2-factor) coverage design-of-experiments, then 9 minimum scenarios are generated, each covering unique pairs, and traceability links each scenario to a specific ODD environment-variable combination in the traceability matrix.
findings.scenario_data:
| Coverage Type | # Scenarios | Features Tested | 3-Way Interactions | Time to Generate |
|----------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Manual Script | 50 | All | 100% | 4 days |
| Pairwise (2-factor) | 9 | All pairs | 0% | 30 min |
| Recommended (3-factor) | 27 | All pairs + key triples | 80% | 1.5 hrs |
findings.reasoning: Pairwise coverage is efficient but misses 3-way interactions (e.g., radar + high sea state + fog). These compound conditions may reveal non-linear robustness degradation. Full factorial is infeasible; a balanced approach is needed.
findings.recommendation: Use pairwise as the baseline (9 scenarios for quick feedback) but identify and augment with hand-picked 3-way combinations (e.g., sea-state × visibility × sensor fallback). Implement a 'coverage gap report' tool in Scenic that flags uncovered triples.

findings.finding_title: Missing acronym expansion: PSRQ introduced without first-use definition
findings.current_usage: Section 7 header 'Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ)' defines the term in narrative, but earlier references in sections 1–6 (Problem statement, User story, Outcome, Acceptance criteria) use 'PSRQ' without expansion, assuming reader familiarity
findings.proposed_definition: Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ) — the formal evaluation campaign comprising seven phases (requirements decomposition, data curation, model-layer evaluation, system-layer evaluation, real-world validation, safety case & Article 36, in-service monitoring) that an USV perception stack must pass before clearance for sea trials and operational release.
findings.reasoning: IEEE and UK MOD standards require acronym expansion at first use. While PSRQ is defined in Section 7, readers of sections 1–6 (which may be distributed separately) encounter it without expansion.
findings.recommendation: Expand PSRQ in Section 3 (Problem statement) with inline definition: 'the Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ), a formal evaluation campaign...'. Maintain definition in Section 7 for reference.

findings.finding_title: Closed-loop simulation vs. most physical trials — quantification missing
findings.text_excerpt: Closed-loop simulation replaces most physical trials.
findings.interpretation_a: Simulation replaces ≥80% of planned physical trials, reducing sea-trial days from [X] to [Y]
findings.interpretation_b: Simulation replaces routine regression tests; only integration/validation trials remain physical
findings.interpretation_c: 'Most' is rhetoric; no specific target is intended
findings.reasoning: This is an optimisation claim (Section 8) but lacks a baseline. How many physical trials would be run without simulation? What is the cost savings? This affects Phase 5 resource planning and the decision to proceed with synthetic-first testing.
findings.recommendation: Quantify: 'Historical Naval USV T&E requires ≥20 days sea trials per programme. AI pipeline executes 85% of scenarios in Phase 4 closed-loop simulation (digital twin + synthetic sensor data). Phase 5 physical trials are reduced to ≤3 days (validation sampling only), reducing schedule from [X] weeks to [Y] weeks.'

D.finding_title: Undefined term: 'declared ODD states' — no operational boundary definition
D.current_usage: Acceptance criteria demand 'Coverage of declared ODD states ≥ 95%' but no mechanism specifies which states count as 'declared' or how they are formally bounded
D.proposed_definition: The set of operational design domain conditions explicitly specified in the USV capability document (weather ranges, sensor modes, traffic density classes, geographic boundaries, speed envelopes) which a test campaign must demonstrate coverage against
D.reasoning: Without a formal definition of what constitutes a 'declared' ODD state, the 95% coverage metric becomes unmeasurable. Are weather bounds enumerated? Is traffic density a continuous variable or a set of classes? How are edge cases (e.g., extreme fog at high speed) classified?
D.recommendation: Reference or create a normative ODD boundary document (e.g., JSP or MOD classification) that formally lists declared environmental dimensions, their ranges, discrete states, and any exclusions. Update traceability matrix to map each declared state to test evidence.

D.finding_title: Undefined term: 'classes' in adversarial robustness context — no business rule for partitioning
D.current_usage: Acceptance criteria require 'Adversarial robustness bounds filed for every class' but the term 'class' is never defined: does it mean object types (ship, buoy, obstacle)? Environmental states (fog, rain)? Sensor modalities?
D.proposed_definition: A distinct partition of the object-detection or collision-avoidance problem space for which separate adversarial robustness margins are computed and certified. Examples: maritime vessel classes (commercial, military, fishing), environmental degradation modes (fog, rain, low-light), or sensor failure scenarios (lidar dropout, camera blur).
D.reasoning: Without defining how classes partition the problem space, it is impossible to enumerate robustness bounds, plan testing effort, or audit coverage. A team might define 3 classes; another might define 30.
D.recommendation: In Phase 1 (Requirements decomposition), create a normative classification schema aligned with threat models and operational scenarios. Use object taxonomies from training datasets (e.g., AUVLAB) as the baseline. Document how object classes map to adversarial test cases and robustness margins.

D.finding_title: Undefined term: 'environment variables' — no list, units, or monitoring thresholds
D.current_usage: Acceptance criteria state 'Drift monitors live on ≥ 3 environment variables post-deployment' but does not specify which variables (wind speed? visibility? sea state?), units, or what constitutes a drift alarm
D.proposed_definition: Measurable environmental parameters sampled from sensor telemetry and environmental data that define the ODD (e.g., wind speed in knots, visibility in metres, sea state Beaufort scale, ambient illumination in lux, water temperature in Celsius) monitored in production to detect shifts beyond declared ODD bounds.
D.reasoning: Without a defined list and alarm thresholds, post-deployment monitoring cannot be implemented or audited. Teams will arbitrarily select variables, making drift detection inconsistent and unmeasurable.
D.recommendation: Create an environment-variable registry mapping ODD dimensions to telemetry sources, units, and acceptable ranges. Define drift thresholds aligned with AMLAS re-qualification triggers. Implement in Evidently AI or Arize monitoring with alert escalation to NavyX and RAISO.

D.finding_title: Ambiguous quantifier: 'rare events' — no frequency or probability threshold
D.current_usage: Phase 2 data curation states 'synthetic augmentation for rare events' but does not define what frequency qualifies an event as 'rare' or what synthetic coverage target is acceptable
D.proposed_definition: Operational scenarios with historical occurrence frequency < 5% in available real-world datasets or weather states occurring in < 10% of UK littoral operating hours, requiring synthetic generation to achieve test coverage thresholds
D.reasoning: Without frequency thresholds, teams may disagree on which events require synthetic augmentation, leading to inconsistent test coverage. 'Rare' is subjective: fog in northern waters? Equipment failure? Simultaneous sensor occlusion?
D.recommendation: Establish a quantitative rare-event taxonomy in Phase 1, with frequency bins (< 1%, 1-5%, 5-10%) mapped to synthetic generation budget. Align with AUVLAB and MOD sea-trial datasets to establish baseline frequency distributions.

D.term: test vs. trial vs. evaluation vs. qualification
D.current_usage: Document mentions 'sea trials' (Phase 5), 'evaluation campaign' (Phase 3–4), 'qualification' (PSRQ acronym), and generic 'testing' throughout, but does not define boundaries between simulation-based evaluation, lab testing, and real-world validation
D.proposed_definition: Test: repeatable, controlled execution of a specific scenario in simulation (lab, digital twin). Trial: real-world execution with controlled instrumentation. Evaluation: comparative analysis of test/trial results against acceptance criteria. Qualification: formal certification that a system meets a requirement, documented in a safety case.
D.reasoning: Ambiguity between 'test' and 'trial' makes it unclear what counts as certification evidence. Is a simulation test equivalent to a sea trial? The PSRQ 'evaluation campaign' spans multiple phases; are all evidence types equally rigorous?
D.recommendation: Define a normative evidence hierarchy in the PSRQ Phase 1 decomposition: (1) HIL/simulation-based testing, (2) lab validation, (3) controlled trials, (4) operational trials. Assign evidence weights for safety-case arguments. Update acceptance criteria to specify which phases produce certificable evidence.

D.finding_title: Undefined term: 'degraded-sensor modes' — no failure model or performance bounds
D.current_usage: Phase 4 (System-layer evaluation) includes 'degraded-sensor modes' but does not specify which sensors, what degradation (noise, latency, partial loss, full loss?), or acceptable collision-avoidance performance under degradation
D.proposed_definition: Sensor failure scenarios where one or more perception modalities (radar, visual camera, infrared, LiDAR) experience reduced fidelity or temporary unavailability, including partial occlusion, noise amplification, frame-rate reduction, or signal dropout. Collision-avoidance behaviour must remain safe within defined degradation envelopes.
D.reasoning: Without enumerating failure modes (e.g., camera blur at 100m range, radar rain clutter, LiDAR fogging), test coverage is ambiguous. Teams may test only minor degradations while critical failure scenarios go unvalidated.
D.recommendation: Develop a sensor-failure taxonomy in Phase 1 aligned with DEFSTAN 00-056 (safety management). For each sensor, define: (a) failure modes (noise, latency, dropout), (b) acceptable performance bounds during degradation, (c) handover thresholds to human-on-the-loop. Map each scenario to Phase 4 HIL tests.

D.finding_title: Ambiguous quantifier: 'human-on-the-loop handovers' — no definition of operator decision criteria or safety bounds
D.current_usage: Phase 4 (System-layer evaluation) requires testing 'human-on-the-loop handovers' but does not define: when should the USV trigger a handover? What time window is acceptable for human response? What happens if the human does not respond in time?
D.proposed_definition: Scenarios in which the USV autonomously detects that a situation exceeds its operational envelope (ODD violation, confidence < threshold, novel obstacle, comms loss) and initiates a controlled transition to human operator control. Includes operator notification latency, acceptance criteria for state transfer, and fallback safety actions if human takeover is not confirmed within a time threshold.
D.reasoning: Without defining handover criteria and time bounds, Phase 4 HIL tests cannot be designed or validated. Is a 2-second handover window acceptable? What ODD violations trigger handover? How is safety guaranteed if the human does not respond?
D.recommendation: In Phase 1 (Requirements decomposition), define handover rules using a decision table: (a) ODD violation triggers (enumerated), (b) confidence thresholds, (c) notification method and latency SLA, (d) operator response deadline, (e) autonomous safety action (e.g., emergency stop) if deadline is missed. Update Phase 4 HIL tests to verify handover timings under communication delays and operator workload.

D.finding_title: Incomplete definition: 'coverage ≥ 95%' — no specification of measurement basis or sub-categories
D.current_usage: Acceptance criteria state 'Coverage of declared ODD states ≥ 95%' but coverage of what? Is it (a) number of declared ODD states tested? (b) volume of ODD hyperspace explored? (c) equivalence classes derived from requirements? (d) object-detection classes per threat model?
D.proposed_definition: Coverage is measured across four dimensions: (1) ODD state coverage: percentage of declared environmental condition combinations exercised in test (e.g., 95% of weather × visibility × traffic-density combinations); (2) requirement coverage: percentage of requirements linked to test cases in traceability matrix; (3) object-class coverage: percentage of adversarial robustness bounds computed per object or failure class; (4) code/decision-tree coverage: percentage of object-detection model branches or collision-avoidance decision paths exercised.
D.reasoning: A 95% ODD state coverage metric is only meaningful if the measurement basis is defined. Teams may report different coverage percentages using different metrics, making audit and certification impossible.
D.recommendation: In Phase 1 (Requirements decomposition), specify coverage measurement methods: (a) enumerate ODD dimensions and create a coverage matrix, (b) define equivalence classes for each dimension (e.g., weather: clear, rain, fog; visibility: > 1km, 0.5-1km, < 0.5km), (c) assign weights if not all combinations are equally critical, (d) integrate CVAT/FiftyOne coverage metrics, (e) link to traceability matrix in MLflow/DVC. Report coverage sub-categories in evidence bundle.

D.finding_title: Compound concept collision: 'safety case' used without distinction between generic and AMLAS-compliant variants
D.current_usage: Acceptance criteria require 'GSN safety case draft auto-generated'; Phase 6 requires 'AMLAS-structured evidence bundle, GSN safety case'; but no distinction is made between a GSN argument structure and an AMLAS-compliant safety case (which requires specific artefacts: hazard log, V&V matrix, DNN robustness records, drift monitoring baseline)
D.proposed_definition: Generic safety case: a GSN argument with evidence supporting safety claims. AMLAS-compliant safety case: a structured safety case built on AMLAS activities, including: (1) hazard and risk analysis, (2) specification of acceptable performance, (3) design documentation, (4) verification and validation artefacts, (5) assurance of the ML components (training data, model card, robustness bounds, explainability records), (6) deployment and monitoring plan.
D.reasoning: Auto-generating a GSN structure is not the same as building an AMLAS-compliant safety case. The latter requires substantive ML-specific artefacts (model cards, robustness matrices, drift baselines) that cannot be auto-generated from test results alone.
D.recommendation: Clarify in Phase 6 acceptance criteria: (a) GSN auto-generation outputs a skeleton structure (claims, strategies, context nodes); (b) human safety engineers populate AMLAS-required artefacts (hazard log, training-data lineage, robustness bounds); (c) evidence bundle includes both GSN and AMLAS ML-specific records. Reference AdvoCATE or D-Case Editor for GSN tooling; define ML artefact templates.

D.finding_title: Missing acronym expansion: PSRQ introduced without first-use definition
D.current_usage: Section 7 header 'Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ)' defines the term in narrative, but earlier references in sections 1–6 (Problem statement, User story, Outcome, Acceptance criteria) use 'PSRQ' without expansion, assuming reader familiarity
D.proposed_definition: Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ) — the formal evaluation campaign comprising seven phases (requirements decomposition, data curation, model-layer evaluation, system-layer evaluation, real-world validation, safety case & Article 36, in-service monitoring) that an USV perception stack must pass before clearance for sea trials and operational release.
D.reasoning: IEEE and UK MOD standards require acronym expansion at first use. While PSRQ is defined in Section 7, readers of sections 1–6 (which may be distributed separately) encounter it without expansion.
D.recommendation: Expand PSRQ in Section 3 (Problem statement) with inline definition: 'the Perception Stack Robustness Qualification (PSRQ), a formal evaluation campaign...'. Maintain definition in Section 7 for reference.

F.finding_title: In-service monitoring decomposes into multiple distinct capabilities
F.feature_name: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
F.business_story: As an In-Service Monitor, I want to deploy live drift detection on ≥3 environment variables using Evidently AI, Alibi Detect and Arize, So that I can trigger re-qualification and model updates automatically post-deployment.
F.reasoning: Section 7.7 mentions 'Drift detection, continuous re-qualification, automatic update of the evidence store on model change' but groups these as a single phase. At minimum, this decomposes into: (1) drift detection on declared variables, (2) canary deployment / staged rollout, (3) alarm escalation to the on-call team, (4) re-qualification trigger, (5) evidence store synchronization. Each requires distinct operators, policies and tooling.
F.recommendation: Decompose Phase 7 into sub-features: Drift detection, Canary deployment orchestration, Alert escalation, Re-qualification triggering, and Evidence store auto-sync. Define SLOs for each.

F.finding_title: Sensor log ingestion pathway is implied but not explicitly named
F.feature_name: Sensor log ingestion and real-world data curation
F.business_story: As a Data Engineer, I want to ingest and curate real sensor logs from AUVLAB, MOD sea-trial tapes, and UK maritime corpora, So that I can build an authoritative, classified-capable real-world baseline for model evaluation.
F.reasoning: Section 7.2 mentions 'real sensor logs (AUVLAB + MOD sea-trial tapes) plus synthetic augmentation' but does not explicitly name a feature for log ingestion, parsing, or curation. The pipeline must connect external classified datasets; this ingestion capability is load-bearing.
F.recommendation: Explicitly name and define the data ingestion feature in requirements. Specify formats (ROS bag, NetCDF, etc.), classification handling, and integration with cloud storage (DAIS, Crown Hosting).

F.finding_title: Traceability of AI-generated tests to requirements is critical but feature is not fully scoped
F.business_story: As a Verification Manager, I want every AI-generated test case to be automatically traceable to a named requirement in the traceability matrix, So that I can demonstrate end-to-end coverage to auditors and maintain a defensible chain of custody.
F.reasoning: Acceptance criterion 6.5 states 'All AI-generated tests traceable to a named requirement.' The feature is named in the criterion but not elaborated in the PSRQ phases. The pipeline must ingest the traceability matrix from Phase 1, associate every synthetic scenario with one or more requirements, and report coverage gaps. This is a cross-cutting analytics feature.
F.recommendation: Explicitly add a feature for Coverage Analytics using CVAT / FiftyOne / Labelbox. Define traceability schema (requirement ID → test case ID → evidence URL). Integrate with CI/CD to fail the build if coverage < 95%.

F.finding_title: Synthetic data provenance and sovereign policy enforcement is an unnamed administrative feature
F.feature_name: Synthetic data provenance audit and sovereign policy enforcement
F.business_story: As a Compliance Officer, I want to enforce the sovereign synthetic-data provenance policy and audit the provenance of every synthetic training and test image, So that I can ensure alignment with AUKUS Pillar II and UK Defence AI Playbook obligations.
F.reasoning: Section 10 mentions 'Sovereign synthetic-data provenance policy' as a non-AI enabler, but the pipeline itself must implement and audit this policy. Who validates that StyleGAN and diffusion models did not train on foreign datasets? How is provenance metadata stored and reported? This is a compliance and governance feature, not just a policy statement.
F.recommendation: Define a feature for Synthetic Data Provenance Auditing. Implement metadata tagging (data origin, generation seed, model version, approval timestamp). Add a dashboard and export for MOD audits. Integrate with the safety case evidence bundle.

F.finding_title: Cross-cutting reporting and visualization feature is absent
F.business_story: As a Programme Manager, I want the pipeline to generate real-time dashboards of coverage, robustness bounds, drift signals, and evidence completeness, So that I can report progress to Navy Command, Dstl and RAISO / DAU decision-makers.
F.reasoning: The requirements demand 'a defensible AMLAS-compliant safety case' and 'traceable, reproducible evidence,' but do not specify who consumes this evidence or how it is visualized for stakeholders. A Programme Manager cannot read raw JSON; they need dashboards. Reporting is a critical cross-cutting concern spanning all PSRQ phases.
F.recommendation: Add a feature for Real-Time Evidence Dashboarding. Specify KPIs (ODD coverage %, adversarial robustness bounds, drift alarm rate, re-qualification backlog). Integrate with JIRA / Confluence for stakeholder briefs. Support export to PowerPoint and PDF for formal reviews.

F.finding_title: Human-on-the-loop operating concept requires explicit feature design
F.business_story: As a System Operator, I want the pipeline to escalate ambiguous or high-risk perception decisions to a human for override, and log all human interventions with justification, So that I can maintain safety assurance and regulatory accountability in closed-loop scenarios.
F.reasoning: Section 7.4 mentions 'human-on-the-loop handovers' and Section 10 mentions 'Human-on-the-loop operating concept with defined escalation,' but neither the PSRQ phases nor the AI tooling section define the feature. This is critical for Phase 4 (System-layer evaluation) and Phase 7 (post-deployment). Requires UI, logging, decision-model integration.
F.recommendation: Add a feature for Human-on-the-Loop Escalation. Define decision thresholds (e.g., confidence < 0.7 → escalate), UI/UX for handover, timeout policies, and integration with the evidence store. Test with actual operators in Phase 4.

O.finding_title: Drift detection alarm raised and escalated to re-qualification team
O.outcome_description: When drift is detected on one of ≥3 monitored environment variables post-deployment, an alarm is raised via Evidently AI / Alibi Detect, logged, and escalated to the In-Service Monitor with trigger for manual or automatic re-qualification.
O.triggering_scenario: Deployed model exhibits significant data distribution shift on monitored environment variables (e.g., sea state, lighting, sensor noise) exceeding configured drift threshold.
O.reasoning: The requirements explicitly state 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables post-deployment' and Phase 7 specifies automatic re-qualification triggers. This is a critical safety outcome in a maritime autonomous system.
O.recommendation: Define drift threshold limits and escalation protocol (email, Slack, monitoring dashboard). Specify which team/individual receives alarm and within what SLA.

O.finding_title: Adversarial robustness bound violation triggers CI gate failure
O.outcome_description: When a model's adversarial robustness score for any object class falls below the filed bound, the CI/CD pipeline gate fails, blocking model deployment and routing evidence to safety review.
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 3 evaluation shows adversarial robustness below threshold for pedestrian, vessel, or obstacle class during model rebuild or synthetic data augmentation.
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion specifies 'Adversarial robustness bounds filed for every class' and CI/CD integration requirement indicates automated gate enforcement. This protects against certification bypass.
O.recommendation: Operationalize 'robustness bound violation' → gate fail → human safety review. Define remediation path (retrain, data augmentation, class re-weighting).

O.finding_title: Article 36 evidence bundle exported and submitted for legal review
O.outcome_description: Once the GSN safety case is approved (or conditionally approved), a machine-readable evidence bundle covering collision-avoidance decision logic, adversarial robustness bounds, and explainability evidence is exported in a format compliant with UK legal review protocols and submitted to the Article 36 reviewer (external legal/policy office).
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 6 completion: approved GSN safety case + locked evidence store + confirmed compliance with DEFSTAN 00-056, JSP 520, JSP 936.
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion and Phase 6 explicitly name Article 36 review. This is a critical regulatory gate. No explicit response outcome defined (approval, conditional, rejection).
O.recommendation: Clarify expected SLA for Article 36 review (e.g., 4 weeks), response types (approved, conditionally approved pending changes, rejected), and escalation contacts (DAU, RAISO, MOD legal).

O.finding_title: Synthetic data poisoning detected; model flagged for human inspection and rollback
O.outcome_description: When a synthetic training sample exhibits adversarial properties or causes model performance regression (≥2% accuracy drop on held-out real data), the sample is flagged, the model checkpoint is marked suspect, and rollback to the last certified version is triggered with human safety review mandatory.
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 2 data curation → synthetic augmentation → Phase 3 model training. StyleGAN-generated or diffusion-rendered sample degrades generalization or fits an adversarial pattern.
O.reasoning: Synthetic data is a critical risk: GANs can generate mode-collapsed or unrealistic samples. Undetected poisoning undermines the evidence case. No explicit requirement found for this, making it a hanging outcome.
O.recommendation: Implement synthetic data validation: held-out real test set monitoring, adversarial detection on synthetic batch, ablation testing (train with/without each synthetic batch). Define rollback SOP.

O.finding_title: Article 36 review approval granted; operational clearance pathway opens
O.outcome_description: Article 36 reviewer approves the collision-avoidance evidence bundle. This triggers authorization for sea trials (Phase 5 completion) and, contingent on RAISO/DAU sign-off, operational release of the USV perception stack.
O.triggering_scenario: Article 36 reviewer receives evidence bundle, conducts legal/humanitarian review of autonomous collision-avoidance logic, finds no conflict with international humanitarian law or weapons law.
O.reasoning: This is the main gate between PSRQ (Phases 1–6) and operational deployment. Unambiguous approval enables Phase 7 (in-service monitoring).
O.recommendation: Coordinate approval with RAISO and DAU. Establish sign-off chain of custody. Document conditions on operational release (e.g., monitor drift on 5 variables not 3, escalate all alarms to Dstl).

O.finding_title: Article 36 review rejection or conditional approval; remediation evidence required
O.outcome_description: Article 36 reviewer identifies gaps, inconsistencies, or insufficient evidence in the collision-avoidance decision logic. Review outcome is conditional (approval pending specific evidence additions/changes) or outright rejection, blocking operational release until remediation is complete.
O.triggering_scenario: Article 36 review identifies: gap in explainability of target classification (merchant vessel vs. fishing net), insufficient adversarial robustness evidence for night scenarios, or ambiguity in collision-avoidance rule logic.
O.reasoning: Requirements do not specify remediation path or escalation protocol if Article 36 review fails. This is a critical hanging outcome in a military/legal context.
O.recommendation: Pre-establish dialogue with Article 36 reviewer. Define evidence types they will accept. Create fast-track rework cycle (e.g., add Grad-CAM explainability, re-run adversarial robustness on night subset, clarify rule logic). Assign RAISO as mediator.

O.finding_title: Human-on-the-loop handover scenario fails in HIL; collision-avoidance rule logic revised
O.outcome_description: Phase 4 system-layer HIL evaluation tests human-on-the-loop handover scenarios (e.g., model detects ambiguous target, autonomy requests human takeover). If handover logic fails (operator unable to assume safe control in time, or model fails to escalate), the collision-avoidance decision rule is marked unsafe, logged as a critical finding, and routed to safety review for rule re-design.
O.triggering_scenario: HIL test: model encounters high-confidence but ambiguous detection (e.g., 50/50 merchant vessel vs. floating debris), should escalate. Escalation handler fails or human response time exceeds safe threshold (e.g., >2 seconds).
O.reasoning: Human-on-the-loop is a core requirement (Phase 4) but failure path is not defined. In military context, escalation failure is a critical safety event requiring immediate evidence collection and rule redesign.
O.recommendation: Define handover SOP: model confidence threshold for escalation, escalation message format, human operator acknowledgment timeout (e.g., 1.5s), safe state if no response (e.g., emergency stop). Log all handovers + human responses in evidence store.

O.finding_title: Traceability matrix open and requirement-to-test links established in Phase 1
O.outcome_description: Phase 1 requirements decomposition produces an open traceability matrix linking each ODD state, safety requirement, and mission requirement to one or more test cases. All AI-generated tests are tagged with the requirement ID and test design technique (e.g., boundary value analysis, adversarial mutation). Matrix is version-controlled and updated as evidence is produced.
O.triggering_scenario: Initiation of PSRQ Phase 1. T&E Engineer uses OpenRequirements DeFOSPAM + 106-technique catalogue to decompose requirements.
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion requires 'All AI-generated tests traceable to a named requirement.' RTM is foundational for AMLAS evidence chain and Certification Authority audit.
O.recommendation: Implement RTM as a live document (e.g., Sharepoint, Confluence, DVC). Auto-populate from requirements tool + test generation tool. Run coverage checks: every requirement must have ≥1 test, every test must link to ≥1 requirement.

O.finding_title: ODD coverage target met (≥95%) triggers evidence freeze and safety case advancement
O.outcome_description: When test coverage of declared ODD states reaches ≥95% across physics-accurate sim and synthetic data, the evidence baseline is frozen, traceability matrix locked, and GSN draft safety case preparation authorized.
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 2 data curation + Phase 3 model evaluation accumulates sufficient coverage across all ODD conditions (weather, sensor type, traffic density, lighting, speed ranges).
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion explicitly states 'Coverage of declared ODD states ≥ 95%'. This triggers transition from test design (PSRQ Phase 2–3) to safety case authoring (PSRQ Phase 6).
O.recommendation: Automate coverage metric calculation in CI/CD. Define 'evidence freeze' process: lock test dataset, version all model checkpoints, trigger GSN draft generation.

O.finding_title: GSN safety case draft auto-generated; human reviewer approval or rejection
O.outcome_description: AdvoCATE / D-Case Editor / LLM-GSN auto-generates a draft GSN safety case from AMLAS-structured evidence bundles. The draft is presented to a human Safety Case Author for approval (case adopted, reviewed by Certification Authority) or rejection (case returned for rework, evidence gap identified).
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 6: evidence bundle locked, coverage ≥95%, adversarial robustness bounds filed, explainability reports generated. LLM-GSN is invoked.
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion states 'GSN safety case draft auto-generated and human-approved.' The hanging outcome is: what happens if human rejects the draft? No explicit remediation path defined.
O.recommendation: Define rejection workflow: auto-identify root cause (missing evidence, weak argument, unmet bound), suggest fix (retrain, add test case, gather explainability), resubmit. Track rejection rate and loop time.

O.finding_title: Model rebuild triggered by code change; evidence store auto-updated with new lineage
O.outcome_description: A model code change (e.g., architecture tuning, loss function update) or new training data triggers a rebuild via CI/CD. MLflow / DVC / Weights & Biases automatically logs lineage (data hash, hyperparameters, training date), runs Phase 3 evaluation, and auto-updates the evidence store with new model version metadata and re-baseline drift monitors.
O.triggering_scenario: Developer commits model code to Git; CI/CD pipeline detects change, triggers training and evaluation on held-out test set.
O.reasoning: Phase 7 and MLOps integration requirement explicitly call for automatic evidence store updates on model change. This ensures continuous auditability and re-qualification readiness.
O.recommendation: Configure CI/CD to: (1) version model + data + hyperparameters, (2) run full Phase 3 evaluation, (3) log results to evidence store with timestamp, (4) reset drift monitors if performance meets acceptance criteria.

O.finding_title: Explainability evidence (SHAP, LIME, Grad-CAM) generated and stored for all model classes
O.outcome_description: Phase 3 evaluation generates explainability evidence for each object class (e.g., pedestrian, vessel, buoy, debris) using SHAP values, LIME local approximations, and Grad-CAM saliency maps. Evidence is automatically archived in the evidence store, indexed by model version and class, and made available for Article 36 legal review and Certification Authority audit.
O.triggering_scenario: Model training completes; Phase 3 evaluation suite runs explainability module on held-out test set (≥100 images per class).
O.reasoning: Requirements explicitly call for explainability as a Phase 3 deliverable and as evidence for stakeholder audit. This is non-negotiable for safety-critical military autonomous systems.
O.recommendation: Automate explainability generation in CI/CD. Define coverage: every class × every model version. Store as structured JSON (feature importance, pixel saliency) + visualizations (PNG). Include uncertainty bounds.

O.finding_title: ODD coverage shortfall (<95%) requires synthetic data gap filling
O.outcome_description: When coverage of declared ODD states is <95%, the test architecture identifies coverage gaps and routes a regeneration request to the synthetic data pipeline (NVIDIA TAO / StyleGAN / diffusion), with gap tags appended to the traceability matrix.
O.triggering_scenario: After Phase 3 evaluation, coverage gap analysis shows <95% coverage in identified ODD cells (e.g., fog at night + high sea state + small target).
O.reasoning: Acceptance criterion defines 95% as the must-achieve target. Gap closing is essential for AMLAS evidence completeness and traceability chain.
O.recommendation: Implement automated coverage gap detector. Classify gaps by: criticality (safety-relevant vs. nice-to-have), effort (sim-time to generate), and ODD cell signature.

O.finding_title: Calibration check fails; model confidence threshold adjusted or retrain triggered
O.outcome_description: Phase 3 evaluation detects poor calibration (e.g., model assigns 90% confidence to predictions with only 75% empirical accuracy). Calibration failure triggers either: (a) confidence threshold post-hoc adjustment (Platt scaling, isotonic regression) with evidence of impact, or (b) model retrain with focal loss or temperature scaling.
O.triggering_scenario: Held-out test set evaluation shows calibration error (ECE / MCE) > acceptable threshold (e.g., 5%).
O.reasoning: Calibration is critical for closed-loop collision-avoidance: miscalibrated confidence can lead to unsafe trust-of-AI decisions. Requirements specify 'calibration' as a Phase 3 metric but do not define failure path.
O.recommendation: Define calibration acceptance threshold (e.g., ECE < 3%). On failure: try post-hoc fix first (faster), measure impact on robustness, document in evidence. If post-hoc insufficient, trigger retrain with calibration loss.

O.finding_title: Red-team adversarial scenario uncovers edge case; test case added to coverage
O.outcome_description: Garak / PyRIT red-teaming identifies an adversarial scenario (e.g., lens flare + fast-moving small target + high sea state) where model confidence is high but accuracy is low. The scenario is logged, a synthetic data generation request is issued to StyleGAN/diffusion, new test cases are added to Phase 3, and the traceability matrix is updated with a new requirement node (e.g., 'REQ-EDGE-CASE-0047').
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 3 red-teaming suite generates ~100 adversarial scenario mutations. One causes model failure. Threat modeller flags it.
O.reasoning: Red-teaming is explicitly required but no feedback loop is defined. Edge case discovery should drive coverage closure and improve evidence robustness.
O.recommendation: Implement red-teaming → evidence loop: tag discovered edge cases with 'root cause' (e.g., training data gap, model architecture limitation), generate synthetic samples, retrain, re-evaluate. Measure loop closure time.

O.finding_title: Physical sea trial data analysis reveals sim-to-real gap; synthetic data regeneration prioritized
O.outcome_description: Phase 5 targeted physical trials anchor synthetic evidence to real weather, comms, and UK littoral traffic. Real-world data analysis identifies a sim-to-real mismatch (e.g., model overconfident on real fishing nets vs. synthetic), triggering a gap analysis. Gap is tagged in the evidence store, StyleGAN/diffusion rendering is tuned to better match real sensor characteristics, and Phase 3 evaluation is re-run on augmented synthetic data.
O.triggering_scenario: Phase 5 physical trials in BUTEC or Portland Harbour. Model detects fishing nets with 40% false negative rate on real data vs. 5% on synthetic data. Analysis links gap to sensor noise + rendering fidelity.
O.reasoning: Phase 5 is explicitly about closing sim-to-real gap, but no automated feedback loop is defined. Gap closure is essential for evidence defensibility.
O.recommendation: After sea trials: (1) run model on real data, (2) compare metrics to synthetic baseline, (3) identify classes/conditions with >5% gap, (4) conduct root-cause analysis (rendering, sensor simulation, lighting), (5) regenerate synthetic data, (6) re-train model, (7) document loop in evidence store.

S.finding_title: Synthetic data provenance chain unverifiable when offline generation omits lineage
S.feature: Synthetic sensor data generation for rare and adversarial conditions
S.scenario_text: Given a batch of 5000 synthetic images generated offline from an uncertified model snapshot, when the evidence curator attempts to import them into the qualification dataset, then the pipeline rejects the batch and reports: synthetic-data-provenance-unverified, and the curator must re-generate the batch with full lineage tracking enabled.
S.scenario_data:
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Batch Size | 5000 images |
| Generation Mode | Offline, uncertified snapshot |
| Lineage Status | Missing |
| Rejection Confidence | 100% |
| Mitigation | Full re-generation with MLflow logging |
S.reasoning: For AMLAS compliance and UK Defence regulatory sign-off, every synthetic training and test frame must be traceable to its generative model, parameters, seed data and timestamp. Offline generation without lineage breaks the chain of evidence and invalidates the qualification.
S.recommendation: Enforce mandatory MLflow/DVC tracking for all synthetic data generation. Integrate lineage verification into the evidence curation pipeline as a hard gate. Document acceptable provenance standards (model ID, version, seed set, generation timestamp, CRC) in the CI/CD policy.

S.finding_title: Adversarial robustness bounds unmet for small-object class triggers ODD restriction
S.feature: Adversarial robustness testing and bounds filing
S.scenario_text: Given the small-boat class (< 5 m) achieves only 62% robust accuracy under realistic maritime perturbations, when the safety threshold (SIL 2 equivalent) requires ≥75% robust accuracy, then the pipeline escalates the finding to a major safety exception and halts certification, and the model owner must retrain or accept a reduced ODD domain (e.g., boats > 7 m only).
S.reasoning: Small maritime objects (fishing boats, RIBs, buoys) present the highest collision risk and smallest detection margin. When robustness bounds fail for this class, the model cannot be certified across the full ODD envelope without explicit constraint (e.g., minimum object size).
S.recommendation: Establish per-class robustness thresholds in AMLAS activity 3.2 evidence gate. Require class-aware SIL assignment and ODD annotation. Document accepted constraints (e.g., 'Small-boat detection disabled below 5 m; human escalation required') in the safety case and GSN argument.

S.finding_title: Real-world validation mismatch uncovers sim-to-real gap in lighting and occlusion physics
S.scenario_text: Given model qualified at 92% mAP on held-out synthetic/AUVLAB mix; deployed and tested on new sea-trial corpus, when the verification engineer evaluates the model on 2000 images from a previously unseen UK coastal trial, then measured mAP drops to 71% due to novel lighting angle, tide-driven reflections, and pier occlusions, and a critical sim-to-real mismatch is flagged; model retraining or ODD tightening is required.
S.scenario_data:
| Corpus | mAP | Domain Shift | Root Cause |
|--------|-----|-------------|----------|
| AUVLAB + Synthetic | 92% | None | Baseline |
| UK Sea-Trial (New) | 71% | Severe | Lighting angle, tidal reflections, pier structures |
S.reasoning: Synthetic data and archived AUVLAB trials may not capture all real-world maritime nuances: sun glint off water, pier shadows, tidal artifacts in radar. Phase 5 targeted trials are essential to anchor sim evidence to reality.
S.recommendation: Mandate Phase 5 real-world validation trials covering ≥3 distinct UK coastal sites with documented environmental conditions. Compare Phase 3 held-out accuracy to Phase 5 sea-trial accuracy; flag >15% mismatch as critical and trigger Phase 3 dataset augmentation or model retraining.

S.finding_title: Model overconfidence under adversarial perturbation breaks calibration trust assumption
S.scenario_text: Given model shows 92% mAP and good calibration (ECE=0.038) on clean test set, when the robustness evaluation applies adversarial patches and measures confidence calibration, then under perturbation, the model maintains high confidence (>0.9) but accuracy drops to 71%; ECE jumps to 0.42, and a critical finding is raised: model is overconfident under adversarial conditions; mitigation required.
S.scenario_data:
| Condition | Accuracy | Confidence | ECE | Calibration Status |
|-----------|----------|-----------|-----|-------------------|
| Clean | 92% | 0.88 ± 0.05 | 0.038 | Well-calibrated |
| Adversarial Patch | 71% | 0.91 ± 0.08 | 0.42 | OVERCONFIDENT |
S.reasoning: Calibration is often assumed to transfer from clean to adversarial domains. This assumption fails: the model's confidence estimates become unreliable under attack, creating false safety assurance in deployed systems.
S.recommendation: Mandate adversarial calibration testing as a Phase 3 gate activity. Measure ECE on ≥3 adversarial perturbation types (L∞, patch, fog). If ECE increases >0.2 under perturbation, require either: (1) post-hoc temperature scaling, (2) retraining with adversarial examples, or (3) ODD constraint documenting the known limitation.

S.finding_title: ODD decomposition gaps expose missing sensor-mode variants
S.feature: Automated requirements decomposition to test conditions
S.scenario_text: Given an ODD requirement silent on LiDAR vs. radar sensor selection trade-offs, when the decomposition engine encounters incomplete environmental state coverage, then the pipeline flags the gap and requests human clarification on sensor-mode assumptions, and decomposition pauses until the engineer provides the missing ODD variant.
S.reasoning: Naval USV perception stacks often operate in multi-sensor fusion mode, but ODD requirements may underspecify which sensor modalities are mandatory, fallback, or contingent on environmental conditions. Gaps in sensor-mode decomposition lead to incomplete test coverage and certification risk.
S.recommendation: Mandate structured ODD templates that explicitly list all sensor configurations (primary, fallback, degraded-mode). Extend Dorothy's glossary to include sensor-mode definitions. Integrate sensor-mode decomposition checks into Phase 1 validation gates.

S.finding_title: One-off sensor dropout during high-complexity encounter masks recovery robustness
S.scenario_text: Given a 3-vessel encounter scenario (crossing traffic + stationary buoy) with radar briefly offline (2 s), when the radar signal is lost during the critical 60–65 s window of the encounter, then the collision-avoidance algorithm reverts to visual + IR detection and maintains safe separation, and recovery time is logged; if >10 s, a minor safety finding is raised for human review.
S.scenario_data:
| Metric | Value |
|--------|-------|
| Encounter Complexity | 3 vessels + buoy |
| Sensor Dropout | Radar, 2 s |
| Loss Timing | 60–65 s (critical phase) |
| Recovery Time | < 10 s (PASS) |
| Fallback Sensors | Visual + IR |
S.reasoning: Transient sensor faults during high-stakes manoeuvres are plausible maritime events (antenna jitter, RF interference). The system must gracefully degrade and recover without triggering collision. Edge case: short dropout windows may not trigger redundancy logic if timing is poor.
S.recommendation: Expand Phase 4 HIL suite to include randomized transient sensor dropouts (100–3000 ms) at encounter-critical time windows. Quantify recovery-time SLO (e.g., 'Full sensor recovery within 5 s'). Add sensor-health assertion checks to the collision-avoidance state machine.

S.finding_title: Human rejection of auto-generated GSN claim exposes inference gaps in safety reasoning
S.feature: AMLAS-structured evidence bundle and GSN safety case auto-generation
S.scenario_text: Given auto-generated GSN claim: 'Model robust under fog (activity 3.2 evidence)' lacks collision-avoidance context, when the safety case author (human) reviews the claim and finds no link to system-layer HIL outcomes, then the human rejects the claim and requests manual refinement, and the pipeline logs the rejection reason and re-tries generation with human-provided context hint.
S.scenario_data:
| Claim | Evidence | Link | Status |
|-------|----------|------|--------|
| 'Robust under fog' | Model-layer robustness (AMLAS 3.2) | Model accuracy only | REJECTED |
| Required Refinement | Must connect model robustness to closed-loop collision avoidance outcome | System-layer integration | PENDING |
S.reasoning: LLM-based safety case generation may produce claims that are evidence-grounded but lack critical cross-layer reasoning. A model may be robust in isolation but poorly integrated into the collision-avoidance decision loop, creating spurious safety arguments.
S.recommendation: Require human-in-the-loop gates for GSN claim generation. Implement multi-stage refinement: (1) auto-generate draft claims, (2) automated cross-layer coherence checks, (3) human safety engineer review. Log rejection patterns to improve LLM prompting.

S.finding_title: Drift monitor SLO boundary at ±10% creates ambiguity in escalation timing
S.feature: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring
S.scenario_text: Given deployment monitoring 3 ODD variables (visibility, sea state, illumination) with SLO ±10% from baseline, when visibility mean drifts from 300 m to 310 m (at 95% CI confidence interval boundary), then the system issues an SLO-yellow alert indicating marginal breach, and if the drift persists for >2 hours, escalates to SLO-red and triggers model snapshot checkpoint.
S.scenario_data:
| Variable | Baseline | Observed | Delta | CI (95%) | Alert Level |
|----------|----------|----------|-------|---------|-------------|
| Visibility | 300 m | 310 m | +3.3% | Within ±10% | Yellow |
| Visibility (Persistent) | 300 m | 315 m (2+ hrs) | +5% | Crossing boundary | Red → Checkpoint |
S.reasoning: SLO thresholds at hard boundaries (±10%) create decision ambiguity near the threshold. Lack of hysteresis or persistence logic can cause alert flapping or delayed escalation during marginal conditions.
S.recommendation: Implement hysteresis and persistence logic in drift detection: (1) SLO-yellow after >1 violation, (2) SLO-red only after >N consecutive 15-min windows above threshold, (3) auto-escalate to checkpoint after >2 hours at SLO-red. Document escalation decision tree in monitoring policy.

S.finding_title: Pairwise scenario coverage achieves 10× productivity gain but misses 3-way interactions
S.feature: OpenSCENARIO 2.0 and Scenic scenario definition and automation
S.scenario_text: Given Scenic defines encounters across sea-state ∈ {Beaufort 2,3,4}, visibility ∈ {50 m,200 m,500 m}, sensor ∈ {radar, lidar, visual}, when the scenario generator applies pairwise (2-factor) coverage design-of-experiments, then 9 minimum scenarios are generated, each covering unique pairs, and traceability links each scenario to a specific ODD environment-variable combination in the traceability matrix.
S.scenario_data:
| Coverage Type | # Scenarios | Features Tested | 3-Way Interactions | Time to Generate |
|----------------|-------------|-----------------|-------------------|-----------------|
| Manual Script | 50 | All | 100% | 4 days |
| Pairwise (2-factor) | 9 | All pairs | 0% | 30 min |
| Recommended (3-factor) | 27 | All pairs + key triples | 80% | 1.5 hrs |
S.reasoning: Pairwise coverage is efficient but misses 3-way interactions (e.g., radar + high sea state + fog). These compound conditions may reveal non-linear robustness degradation. Full factorial is infeasible; a balanced approach is needed.
S.recommendation: Use pairwise as the baseline (9 scenarios for quick feedback) but identify and augment with hand-picked 3-way combinations (e.g., sea-state × visibility × sensor fallback). Implement a 'coverage gap report' tool in Scenic that flags uncovered triples.

P.finding_title: Drift detection on non-monitored environment variable — alert, suppress, or silent log?
P.scenario: Live drift detection and continuous monitoring scenario: deployment monitoring 3 ODD variables (visibility, sea state, illumination) with SLO ±10% from baseline. A fourth unmonitored variable (e.g., sensor frequency response, antenna gain) exhibits significant drift.
P.realistic_alternative: System silently logs drift on non-monitored variable to audit trail; no alert issued, no re-qualification trigger
P.absurd_alternative: System auto-escalates drift on any variable to RAISO with cc: ship's captain, demanding vessel course reversal regardless of variable safety relevance
P.reasoning: Requirements state 'Drift monitors live on ≥3 environment variables' but do not specify whether drift on unmonitored variables is ignored, logged, or escalated. A developer must guess whether to blind-spot unmonitored drift or implement defensive monitoring on all observable variables.
P.recommendation: Explicitly define: (1) which 3 variables are monitored and why, (2) whether drift on non-monitored variables triggers any action (alert, log, suppress), (3) SLA for escalation if unmonitored drift is detected

P.finding_title: GSN safety case draft rejected by human reviewer — remediation timeout and escalation undefined
P.scenario: AMLAS-structured evidence bundle is locked; LLM-GSN auto-generates GSN draft. Safety Case Author reviews draft, finds 3 weak inference links ('model robust under fog' not connected to system-layer collision-avoidance evidence). Author rejects draft. No explicit process defined for remediation.
P.realistic_alternative: Rejected draft is returned to evidence team; automatic loop attempts regeneration with human-provided context hints; if 2nd attempt also rejected, escalates to Safety Review Board with 10-day SLA
P.absurd_alternative: Pipeline auto-downgrades rejected claim to 'suggestion' rather than 'goal' in GSN tree, ships the weakened safety case without human re-approval, auto-logs a compliance exception for post-deployment review
P.reasoning: Acceptance criterion states 'GSN safety case draft auto-generated and human-approved' but does not define: (1) what 'approved' means (pass/fail thresholds on inference strength?), (2) rejection handling (rework loop, escalation, timeout), (3) SLA for re-approval. A developer must guess whether to auto-retry generation, log for manual rework, or escalate.
P.recommendation: Define approval workflow: (1) auto-generated draft triggers automated quality checks (coverage %, inference strength), (2) if auto-checks fail, flag for human review with specific issues identified, (3) rejection routes to evidence team with suggested fixes, (4) max 2 auto-generation retries; 3rd rejection escalates to Safety Review Board, (5) establish SLA (e.g., 5 days to re-approval or escalation)

P.finding_title: Real-world validation mismatch tolerance undefined — 5% vs. 20% performance drop acceptance?
P.scenario: Model-layer accuracy and calibration evaluation: model achieves 92% mAP on held-out synthetic/AUVLAB mix. Phase 5 physical trials test model on 2000 images from new UK coastal trial. Measured mAP is 87%. Sim-to-real gap is 5 percentage points. Is this acceptable or does it require ODD tightening or retraining?
P.realistic_alternative: Pipeline defines acceptable sim-to-real gap as ≤3%; 5% triggers mandatory retraining with synthetic data augmentation focused on identified mismatch class
P.absurd_alternative: Pipeline accepts any gap <25%; 5% is silently logged, model is approved, and gap is noted in fine-print appendix of Article 36 evidence bundle
P.reasoning: Requirements state Phase 5 is 'targeted physical trials to anchor synthetic evidence' but do not define: (1) acceptable tolerance for sim-to-real performance gap, (2) which classes/conditions are safety-critical (small-object detection more critical than large-object?), (3) remediation path if gap exceeds tolerance. A developer must guess whether 5% gap is 'tight enough' or 'unacceptable.'
P.recommendation: Define sim-to-real acceptance criteria: (1) per-class tolerance (e.g., collision-relevant targets ≤2%, non-critical ≤5%), (2) overall model tolerance (e.g., mAP gap ≤3%), (3) confidence calibration tolerance (ECE gap ≤0.05), (4) if gap exceeds tolerance: trigger re-synthesis of gap-relevant samples, retrain model, re-validate in Phase 5, document root cause in evidence store

P.finding_title: Adversarial robustness bound failure mid-deployment — pause, alert, auto-rollback, or silent log?
P.scenario: USV is deployed with filed adversarial robustness bound: 'ship class ≥ 87% recall under L-infinity perturbation ε=8/255.' During operational phase, real-world adversarial conditions are detected (sensor spoofing attempt or unusual EM environment). Model inference on adversarially-perturbed sensor input returns 82% recall, below bound.
P.realistic_alternative: System detects bound violation, immediately pauses autonomous collision-avoidance module, alerts human operator, rolls back to previous certified model, logs incident for post-mission review
P.absurd_alternative: System silently logs bound violation to encrypted audit file; continues autonomous operation at full confidence; escalates incident report to RAISO only on weekly batch digest, no real-time alert
P.reasoning: Requirements state 'Adversarial robustness bounds filed for every class' and CI/CD integration implies automated gate enforcement. But requirements do not specify: (1) what happens if bound is violated in-service, (2) is violation a hard stop (pause autonomy) or soft alert (log + human override option?), (3) escalation SLA. A developer must guess whether bound violation is a safety-critical event or a warning.
P.recommendation: Define in-service bound violation protocol: (1) monitor model inference confidence against filed bounds in real-time, (2) on violation: trigger automatic safe-state transition (reduce speed, request human takeover), (3) log incident with sensor input snapshot for offline analysis, (4) alert human operator within 1 second, (5) continue operation only if operator explicitly overrides (with audit trail), (6) escalate to Dstl Autonomy Lab within 24 hours

P.finding_title: Article 36 review returns 'conditional approval' — deploy during conditions being met or delay?
P.scenario: Article 36 legal reviewer receives evidence bundle. Reviewer responds: 'Approved pending clarification on human escalation logic for high-confidence ambiguous targets. Please provide Grad-CAM evidence for 10 fishing-vessel misclassifications by 2026-05-15.' Can the platform proceed to sea trials while conditions are being met?
P.realistic_alternative: Conditional approval does not authorize sea trials or deployment. System must resolve all conditions and obtain unconditional approval before proceeding to Phase 5 trials. Re-submission deadline is 2026-05-15; if not met, review restarts.
P.absurd_alternative: Platform interprets conditional approval as 'provisional go-ahead'; begins Phase 5 sea trials immediately; collects additional evidence in parallel; if condition deadline is missed, quietly revokes conditional approval without notifying sea-trial team, leading to operating evidence gap
P.reasoning: Requirements state 'Article 36 legal review for collision-avoidance behaviour' and Phase 6 triggers evidence export, but do not specify: (1) types of Article 36 responses (approved, conditional, rejected), (2) conditions under which conditional approval allows deployment, (3) SLA for condition resolution and re-review, (4) escalation if conditions are missed. A developer must guess whether conditional approval is a green light (conditional) or a hold pending full approval.
P.recommendation: Establish Article 36 response taxonomy: (1) Unconditional Approval: deploy authorized immediately, (2) Conditional Approval: identifies specific evidence gaps or clarifications needed, sets deadline (e.g., 4 weeks), blocks deployment until conditions resolved and re-review completed, (3) Rejection: blocks deployment, requires rework, re-submit after remediation. Assign RAISO as coordinator; escalate missed deadlines to MOD legal + Dstl within 48 hours.

P.finding_title: Coverage boundary at exactly 95%: does 94.99% pass or fail acceptance?
P.scenario: ODD coverage metric reaches 94.99% after Phase 3 evaluation. Acceptance criterion requires '≥ 95%'. Does 94.99% pass acceptance or trigger synthetic data regeneration loop?
P.realistic_alternative: Pipeline requires 95.0% exactly (strict boundary); 94.99% fails, forces re-generation of synthetic data to push coverage >95%
P.absurd_alternative: Pipeline downgrades the 95% requirement to 94% mid-evaluation to avoid data regeneration cost, silently updates acceptance criterion in evidence store without stakeholder review
P.reasoning: Requirements state coverage '≥ 95%' but do not define: (1) rounding rules for fractional coverage, (2) tolerance for measurement uncertainty in coverage metric, (3) whether boundary is inclusive or exclusive. A developer must guess whether 94.99% is acceptable, needs rounding up, or triggers rejection.
P.recommendation: Specify: (1) coverage calculation method (e.g., proportion of ODD cells with ≥1 test case), (2) rounding rule (e.g., round to nearest 0.1%, or use ceiling function), (3) measurement uncertainty tolerance (e.g., ±0.5%), (4) behavior if boundary is crossed: auto-escalate or allow re-baseline

P.finding_title: Synthetic data provenance partially verified — accept, reject, or quarantine batch?
P.scenario: Synthetic data generation scenario: a batch of 5000 synthetic images is ingested. StyleGAN provenance is signed and verified; diffusion inpainting subprocess on 1200 images lacks signatures due to interrupted training run. 3800 images are fully signed, 1200 are partially signed.
P.realistic_alternative: Pipeline rejects entire batch; requires regeneration of all 5000 with full lineage tracking enabled
P.absurd_alternative: Pipeline accepts all 5000 images but flags the 1200 as 'unverified' in evidence store; during Article 36 review, legal reviewer silently accepts them as 'best effort' without flagging the provenance gap
P.reasoning: Requirements state synthetic data must be 'linked to parent real frame and transformation parameters' but do not specify: (1) acceptance criteria for provenance completeness (100% signed, or majority-rules?), (2) handling of partial provenance (quarantine, accept with flag, reject), (3) impact on evidence defensibility if provenance is incomplete. A developer must guess whether to accept partial provenance or enforce strict full-lineage requirement.
P.recommendation: Define synthetic data acceptance criteria: (1) all generation steps must be logged and signed (StyleGAN seed, hyperparameters, diffusion model version, inpainting scope), (2) missing provenance segments must be regenerated before use, (3) no partial provenance in final evidence bundle, (4) audit trail must be cryptographically linked to evidence store with timestamp and operator identity

P.finding_title: Requirement ID refactored after test generation — relink tests or orphan them?
P.scenario: Phase 1 traceability matrix generates 250 test cases linked to requirement ID 'REQ-ODD-VISIBILITY-50-500M'. Phase 2 requirements are updated; visibility requirement is split into two separate IDs: 'REQ-ODD-VISIBILITY-50-200M' and 'REQ-ODD-VISIBILITY-200-500M'. What happens to the 250 tests?
P.realistic_alternative: CI/CD pipeline detects requirement ID change, attempts to re-link test cases based on requirement text similarity, flags ambiguous re-links for manual review, orphans tests if re-link confidence < 70%
P.absurd_alternative: Pipeline silently maps all 250 tests to the first new requirement ID (200M), causing 200–500M range to lose coverage evidence; audit trail logs this as 'auto-consolidation' without stakeholder notification
P.reasoning: Requirements do not specify how traceability matrix handles requirement refactoring or ID changes. AMLAS evidence chain demands 1:1 traceability, but a developer must guess whether to auto-relink (with risk of false mapping), manually relink (expensive), or orphan tests (loses coverage evidence).
P.recommendation: Implement requirement change-management protocol: (1) traceability matrix tool tracks requirement ID history (old → new mappings), (2) on ID change, all linked test cases are flagged for re-review, (3) developer must explicitly confirm re-link before tests are re-mapped, (4) orphaned tests trigger coverage gap alert, (5) all re-links are audited and logged with timestamp and operator ID

P.finding_title: Drift monitor false-positive under natural seasonal variation — suppress alert, suppress rule, or cooldown?
